questions.text: Manufacturing of Ammonia by the Haber's process is an?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Endothermic reaction: An endothermic reaction is a reaction in which heat is absorbed. This means that the energy of the products is less than the energy of the reactants. The Haber process is not an endothermic reaction because it releases heat when nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas react to form ammonia.

The Haber process is an exothermic reaction because it releases heat when nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas react to form ammonia. The heat released by the Haber process is used to heat the reaction mixture and to drive the reaction forward. The enthalpy change for the Haber's Process is -92.46 kJ. The negative sign signifies it is an "exothermic reaction".

Irreversible reaction: An irreversible reaction is a reaction that cannot be reversed. This means that the products of the reaction cannot be converted back into the reactants. The Haber process is not an irreversible reaction because the ammonia that is produced can be converted back into nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas.
Slow reaction: A slow reaction is a reaction that takes a long time to complete. The Haber process is not a slow reaction because it is typically completed in a few minutes.

questions.explanation: The manufacturing of ammonia by the Haber's process is an exothermic reaction. The Haber's process is a well-known industrial method for the synthesis of ammonia (NH3) from nitrogen (N2) and hydrogen (H2) gases. The balanced chemical equation for the reaction is: N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3 In this reaction, nitrogen and hydrogen gases react to produce ammonia gas. The forward reaction (from left to right) is exothermic, meaning it releases heat to the surroundings. As a result, the reaction is favored at lower temperatures. The Haber's process is carried out at high pressure and moderate temperature to achieve a good yield of ammonia while considering both the reaction rate and the equilibrium position. By removing the heat generated in the exothermic reaction, the process can be made more efficient and productive.

options.explanation: The forward reaction is favored because increasing pressure shifts the equilibrium towards the side with fewer gas molecules. In the Haber process, N2 + 3H2 ↔ 2NH3, there are four molecules on the reactant side and two on the product side, so the production of ammonia is favored.
The reverse reaction is not favored because it would increase the number of gas molecules, which opposes the effect of increased pressure.
One of the reactions must be favored due to the change in pressure, so this option is incorrect.
Both reactions cannot be favored simultaneously under increased pressure, as the shift must favor the side with fewer gas molecules.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that an increase in pressure favors the forward reaction in the Haber process. This is because the forward reaction results in fewer gas molecules: N2 + 3H2 ↔ 2NH3. With an increase in pressure, the system will adjust to minimize this change by favoring the production of fewer gas molecules, hence the forward reaction is favored. Option B is incorrect as the reverse reaction would increase the number of gas molecules, opposing the pressure increase. Option C is incorrect because the pressure change does affect equilibrium, and therefore one reaction is favored. Option D is incorrect since both reactions cannot be favored at the same time with respect to pressure changes.

questions.text: An exothermic reaction is allowed to reach equilibriuim. If heat energy is removed then, the equilibrium will shift?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: To the product side: This is the correct option. Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, the system will shift in a way to minimize the disturbance. In the case of an exothermic reaction, heat is a product of the reaction. If heat is removed from the system, the equilibrium will shift to the product side to produce more heat and minimize the disturbance.

To the reactant side: This option is incorrect because an exothermic reaction is a reaction that releases heat. If heat is removed from an exothermic reaction, the equilibrium will shift to the product side to produce more heat and minimize the disturbance.

Towards the middle: This option is incorrect because the equilibrium will not shift towards the middle. The equilibrium will shift to either the product side or the reactant side, depending on which side is favored by the disturbance.

None of these: This option is incorrect because one of the options must be correct. The equilibrium will either shift to the product side or the reactant side, depending on which side is favored by the disturbance.

questions.explanation: Correct option is A.The answer is to the product side.Le Chatelier's principle states that when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, the system will shift in a way to minimize the disturbance. In the case of an exothermic reaction, heat is a product of the reaction. If heat is removed from the system, the equilibrium will shift to the product side to produce more heat and minimize the disturbance.Here is a diagram that illustrates how the equilibrium will shift:Initial equilibrium[Products] [Reactants]Heat is removed[Products] ↑ [Reactants]The equilibrium will shift to the product side until the concentration of the products increases enough to offset the loss of heat.

questions.text: Under a cyclic path, internal energy after the complete cycle is the same as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: If a cyclic process is carried out on a system, the internal energy of the system will be the same at the end of the process as it was at the beginning of the process. If the cyclic process is reversible, then the internal energy of the system will be the same at the end of the process as it was at the beginning of the process. If the cyclic process is irreversible, then the internal energy of the system may not be the same at the end of the process as it was at the beginning of the process. However, the internal energy of a system will not be twice the initial value after a complete cycle.

The internal energy of a system is a state function, which means that it depends only on the initial and final states of the system and not on the path taken between those states. For a cyclic process, the system returns to its initial state, so the change in internal energy is zero. This means that the internal energy of the system after a complete cycle is the same as the internal energy of the system at the beginning of the cycle. It is not necessarily equal to the internal heat added to the system during the cycle.

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the internal energy of a system is conserved. This means that the internal energy of a system will be the same at the end of a complete cycle as it was initially, at the beginning of the cycle.

For a cyclic process, the net work done by the system is zero, so the work done by the system over a complete cycle is equal in magnitude but opposite in sign to the work done on the system during the reverse cycle. This means that the net work done by the system over a complete cycle is zero. So, initial work is not the same as internal energy.

questions.explanation: According to the first law of thermodynamics, the internal energy of a system is conserved. This means that the internal energy of a system will be the same at the end of a complete cycle as it was initially, at the beginning of the cycle.

questions.text: Which one of the following is irreversible?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Slow compression of an elastic spring
options.explanation: "Slow compression of an elastic spring" is a reversible process. When you slowly compress an elastic spring, you can reverse the process by slowly releasing the compression, and the spring will return to its original state. The elasticity of the spring allows it to store potential energy when compressed and release it when uncompressed.

options.text: Slow evaporation of a substance in an isolated vessel
options.explanation: "Slow evaporation of a substance in an isolated vessel" is a reversible process. In an isolated vessel, the evaporated substance can be condensed back into its liquid state by cooling the vapor, returning it to its original state. The process is reversible because it can be undone without any net effect on the system.

"Slow compression of a gas" is generally a reversible process. If the compression is slow enough and carried out without significant energy losses, the process can be reversed by slowly expanding the gas back to its original volume, returning it to its initial state. However, if the compression is rapid or accompanied by significant energy losses (e.g., friction or heat transfer to the surroundings), the process may become irreversible.

"A chemical explosion" is an irreversible process. A chemical explosion involves a rapid release of energy, typically through a highly exothermic reaction. Once an explosion occurs, it cannot be reversed to bring the reactants back to their original state. The energy released during the explosion dissipates into the surroundings, and the system irreversibly changes.

questions.explanation: The chemical changes are irreversible. The reactants form products that can be changed back into their original form. The reactants form products that can never be changed back to their original form Options A,B&C are reversible.

questions.text: Which of the following has the highest heat of vaporization?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Ethene, also known as ethylene (C2H4), is a hydrocarbon compound. While it does have a heat of vaporization, it is not particularly high compared to other substances. Ethene molecules are held together by relatively weak London dispersion forces, which do not require as much energy to overcome compared to the intermolecular forces present in water.

Nitrogen gas (N2) is a diatomic molecule commonly found in its gaseous state at room temperature and pressure. While nitrogen gas does have a heat of vaporization, it is relatively low compared to many other substances. Nitrogen molecules are held together by relatively weak nitrogen-nitrogen triple bonds, which do not require as much energy to overcome compared to the intermolecular forces present in water.

options.text: All have the same heat of vaporization
options.explanation: This option suggests that all substances have the same heat of vaporization, which is not true. The heat of vaporization varies greatly depending on the substance. Different substances have different intermolecular forces and molecular structures, leading to different amounts of energy required to transition from liquid to gas phase.

Water has the highest heat of vaporization among the options provided. The heat of vaporization of water is relatively high due to the strong hydrogen bonds between water molecules in the liquid phase. These hydrogen bonds require a significant amount of energy to break, resulting in a high heat of vaporization for water.

questions.explanation: The heat of vaporization is defined as the amount of heat required to convert 1g of a liquid into vapors, without any change in temperature. The heat of vaporization depends on the intermolecular forces in a substance. Water molecules have a strong hydrogen bonding, which accounts for their high heat of vaporization at 40.8 kJ/mol.Ethene (ethylene) and nitrogen have heats of vaporization of 13.5 kJ/mole and 2.79 kJ/mol respectively.

questions.text: Heat and work are equivalent. This means
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: When we supply heat to a body we do work on it
options.explanation: No, the statement "When we supply heat to a body, we do work on it" is not correct. In thermodynamics, heat and work are distinct concepts, and they represent different forms of energy transfer.When we supply heat to a body, we are transferring thermal energy from a higher-temperature object to a lower-temperature object. Heat is the transfer of energy between two systems due to a temperature difference, and it flows spontaneously from hotter regions to colder regions. It is a form of energy transfer that is associated with the random motion of particles within a substance.On the other hand, work is the transfer of energy due to a force acting through a distance. When work is done on a body, energy is transferred to the body to change its state or position. For example, when a force is applied to move an object, work is done on that object.

options.text: When we do work on a body we supply heat to it
options.explanation: When we do work on a body, it can result in an increase in the body's internal energy, which can manifest as an increase in its temperature. In this sense, doing work on a body can lead to the supply of heat to the body.Let's consider an example: Suppose you push a piston into a gas-filled container. By doing so, you compress the gas, and work is done on the gas. The compression of the gas increases its internal energy. According to the first law of thermodynamics (the law of conservation of energy), the work done on the gas is equal to the increase in its internal energy. The internal energy of the gas is associated with the kinetic energy of its particles, and an increase in internal energy corresponds to an increase in the temperature of the gas.Therefore, doing work on a body can indeed supply heat to it indirectly by increasing its internal energy and temperature. However, it's important to note that "supplying heat" typically refers to a direct transfer of thermal energy due to a temperature difference, while "doing work" involves mechanical energy transfer. The two processes can be related in certain situations, as described above, but they are distinct concepts in thermodynamics.

options.text: The temperature of a body can be increased by doing work on it
options.explanation: Yes, the temperature of a body can be increased by doing work on it. When work is done on a body, it can lead to an increase in the body's internal energy, which, in turn, can result in a rise in its temperature.Work done on a body involves transferring energy to the body through mechanical means. This energy is converted into the body's internal energy, which is the sum of the kinetic energy and potential energy of its particles. As the internal energy increases, the average kinetic energy of the particles also increases, leading to a rise in temperature.One common example is compressing a gas in a container. When you compress a gas, you are doing work on it by reducing its volume against an external force. The work done on the gas increases its internal energy, and since temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the gas particles, the temperature of the gas increases as well.It's important to note that the increase in temperature due to work done on the body is a result of the transfer of energy and is not a direct heating process like supplying heat through a temperature difference. Nonetheless, work done on a body can indeed raise its temperature by increasing its internal energy.

options.text: Heat and work are not inter convertible
options.explanation: Heat and work are indeed interconvertible forms of energy. In the field of thermodynamics, the first law, also known as the law of conservation of energy, states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transferred or converted from one form to another. This principle applies to heat and work as well.Heat is a form of energy transfer between two systems or objects due to a temperature difference. When heat flows from a hotter object to a colder object, it increases the internal energy of the colder object, raising its temperature. Conversely, if heat flows from a colder object to a hotter object, the internal energy of the colder object decreases.Work, on the other hand, is energy transfer due to the application of force over a distance. When work is done on a system, it can increase the system's internal energy, causing changes in its state or position.In certain thermodynamic processes, heat and work can be interconverted. For example:In a heat engine, heat is supplied to the engine (Q_in), and work is produced by the engine (W_out). The heat energy is converted into work output, and some of the energy is rejected as waste heat (Q_out).In a heat pump, work is done on the system (W_in), and heat is transferred from a cold reservoir to a hot reservoir (Q_out). In this case, work is used to pump heat "uphill" from a colder region to a warmer region.Overall, the first law of thermodynamics states that the total change in the internal energy of a system is the sum of the heat added to the system and the work done on the system. So, while heat and work are distinct forms of energy transfer, they are interconvertible in certain thermodynamic processes, and both play a crucial role in the study of energy and its transformations.

options.explanation: Internal energy is the sum of all the kinetic and potential energies of the particles within a system. Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. It gives an indication of how hot or cold a body is relative to a reference point (such as the Celsius or Kelvin scale). However, it is not the same as thermal energy, as thermal energy depends on both temperature and the amount of substance present.

questions.text: How many grams of butane must be burnt to heat 10 kg water from 30°C to 100°C? The heat of combustion of butane is −700 kcal/mol and the specific heat of water is 1 cal gm−1 K−1.
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Q = m x c x ΔT where, Q = heat energy required m = mass of the water c = specific heat of water ΔT = change in temperature (final temperature - initial temperature) Given values, m = 10 kg = 10,000 gm (since 1 kg = 1000 gm) c = 1 cal gm-1 K-1 ΔT = 100°C - 30°C = 70°C Plugging in the values,Q = 10,000 gm x 1 cal gm-1 K-1 x 70 K Q = 700,000 cal Now, we need to convert the heat energy from calories to grams of butane. The heat of combustion of butane is given as -700 kcal/mol. To convert this to calories per gram of butane, we need to find the molar mass of butane. The molar mass of butane (C4H10)Molar mass = (4 x atomic mass of carbon) + (10 x atomic mass of hydrogen) = 4 x 12.01 g/mol + 10 x 1.01 g/mol = 48.04 g/mol Now, we can calculate the heat energy released per gram of butane, -700 kcal/mol / 48.04 g/mol ≈ -14.57 kcal/g ≈ -14.57 x 1000 cal/g ≈ -14,570 cal/g Since the heat of combustion is given as a negative value, we use the absolute value for the calculation. So, for every 14,570 cal of heat energy released, we need 1 gram of butane. Finally, the amount of butane needed to produce 700,000 cal is, 700,000 cal / 14,570 cal/g ≈ 48.04 g ≈ 48 g The correct option is A) 58 gm.
